---
title: "Wind & Storm Safety"
format: html
---

# 🌬️ Wind & Storm Safety: Hardening the Envelope

Goal: Secure the building’s exterior "envelope" to prevent structural failure and minimize damage from wind-borne projectiles.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## 🛑 1. Critical Actions

These items are the highest priority for life safety and protecting the structural integrity of the sanctuary.

### One-Time Upgrades (Once)

-   **Hurricane Straps:** Install "hurricane straps" or clips to securely anchor the roof rafters to the wall plates. This ensures the roof stays attached during extreme uplift.
-   **Signage:** Install clear, permanent signage directing the congregation and visitors to designated Sheltered Areas (basements or interior rooms) for tornado safety.
-   **Worst-Case Scenario Plan:** Develop a formal protocol for a tornado approaching specifically during a Sunday service or high-occupancy event.
    -   Who gives the order to move?
    -   Which ushers guide which sections?
    -   How do we assist those with limited mobility?

### Recurring Maintenance (Annually)

-   **Tree Safety Audit:** Have a professional check for dead limbs. Remove "hazard trees" that could fall on the building or power lines.
-   **Roof Inspection:** Check for damaged, loose, or missing shingles. Ensure shingles are rated for high-wind speeds (Class F or H).
-   **Projectile Protocol:** Review the plan for securing or moving exterior items (memorial benches, signage, trash cans, or playground equipment) when a high-wind warning is issued.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## ⚠️ 2. Recommended Protection

These measures provide a significant secondary layer of safety for the building's occupants and internal assets.

### One-Time Upgrades (Once)

-   **Door Reinforcement:** Install heavy-duty deadbolts. For double-entry doors, ensure "flush bolts" are installed at the top and bottom of the "inactive" leaf to prevent doors from blowing open.
-   **Interior Stabilization:** Secure tall furniture (such as heavy bookcases or storage cabinets) to the wall using earthquake-style straps.\
    *Note: High winds can cause enough structural vibration to topple unanchored items, even if the building itself remains standing.*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## ✨ 3. Advanced Resilience

For congregations in high-risk zones seeking maximum protection.

### One-Time Structural Upgrades (Once)

-   **Window Protection:** Apply security window film (8–12 mil thickness) to high-risk glass areas. This keeps glass from shattering inward even if struck by debris.
-   **Impact-Resistant Glass:** Replace standard windows with impact-rated glass designed to withstand high-velocity debris strikes.
-   **Storm Shutters:** Install permanent storm shutters that can be deployed quickly before an event.
-   **Plywood Stockpile:** In hurricane-prone regions, pre-cut and store plywood sheets labeled for specific vulnerable windows.

### Recurring Maintenance (Annually)

-   **Supply Audit:** Annually verify that stockpiled supplies (plywood, fasteners, tools) are in good condition and haven't succumbed to rot or rust.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

### Resource Link

Refer to [FEMA Wind Resilience Guides](https://www.fema.gov/sites/default/files/2020-07/guidelines-wind-vulnerability.pdf) for technical specifications on load paths and wind-resistant construction.
<!-- Refer to [FEMA Wind Resilience Guides](https://www.fema.gov/emergency-managers/risk-management/wind) for technical specifications on load paths and wind-resistant construction. -->
